item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How is this not Keter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That fetus weights twice as much as the Earth, if it ever became uncontained, we would all die. Secrecy would be the least of their concerns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it but I think it could be better. Also, maybe consider upgrading it to keter status rather than Euclid, because I feel like a black hole the size of a baby would be INCREDIBLY dangerous if it is released and nearly impossible to suppress the knowledge of should containment fail, which would constitute a major breach of secrecy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh, it does feel like a knock-off of SCp-231, right down to having become pregnant at a disturbingly young age.

I guess it's an okay SCP but yeah, can't get it out of my head that we already have it and it was done better.

And I don't like asking that because I usually really dislike gratuitous evil actions from the SCP Foundation, but in that case it would be justified so… what about killing the girl? The fetus is still contained but no longer gets the sustenance it needs to grow and presumably eventually dies as well, or at very least stays in its current state. Sucks for the girl but better her and than all of Humanity (which would ALSO include her anyway).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: seeing as this is a baby with the mass of a planet, shouldnt this be keter instead of euclid? if it ever becomes uncontained you can kiss your earth goodbye.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having reread this for the contest, I'm kind of feeling like it's not quite different enough from 231 to warrant an upvote. It's kind of "What if we were nice to 231?" with the shades up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can you imagine the post-partum blues??
+1, but I have a few minor nitpicks:

Should'nt the main text describe SCP-1586-2 as "16-weeks old", instead of 12, since that's the most up-to date data? (Maybe strikethrough 12 and add 16?).

(Since I'm a "Dr" I will antropomorphise the item) Is there a reason they don't keep her sedated or in a coma? Unlike 231, here it would be the safest thing to do. It would remove the unpredictable mood swings and probably slow down her own energy consumption.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I also thought of 231 immediately, but I think this stands on its own regardless. At least we don't have to [DATA EXPUNGED] every 24 hours to keep this pregnancy contained.

My one criticism is to not call the skip "she/her", at least in the body of the article. If the doctor wants to anthropomorphise the anomalous object, that's his business.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"SCP-1586 is contained in a standard humanoid containment cell at Humanoid Containment Site 06-3."

Holy hell, that sentence is redundant. Edit it a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When a woman and a dying white dwarf star love each other very much…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded. I think either there's too much or too little science for what's being conveyed. I love the idea, the execution is pretty good, but there are still a few minor but glaring issues I think need to be resolved before this achieves its true potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For being too heavy or too light?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, despite it seemingly being a black hole, a "black body" is merely anything that absorbs most or all electromagnetic radiation; there are different types, and most of them convert light energy into heat. They've made near-ideal black body objects out of nanotubes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The singularity is straightforward and cool. The lensing seems a reasonable way to justify the calculated mass (although I wish you'd been more specific about what kind of rays they pumped through her to measure it), and perhaps the only feasible way if inertial and gravitational effects are not detected. I have a little trouble with the casual mention of the accretion disc - I think that would count as a distinct phenomenon worthy of elaboration. If the event horizon were actually small enough to fit in a uterus, accretion matter that close would either get pulled in almost instantly, or else it would necessarily be revolving very fast around it. Fast enough to emit X-rays, and/or crazy radiation jets worth mentioning in the article. Without a little more attention to that, I find the fact that they identified it as a disc a little hard to swallow. I'm addicted to science, though, so feel free to ignore me. But neutral for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A hysteroscope is a device similar to a colonoscope that has a light source and fiber optics to allow visualization of the inside of the uterus (which is the correct medical term for the womb). However, there is a risk of puncturing the amniotic sac.

I don't think the author means black as in the color, but black as in black hole, since s/he discusses the gravitational lending effect in the next sentence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not bad.
Have a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now why on Earth would you do that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was ready to upvote, until I got to the line about it already weighing twice as much as the earth, and downvoted instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1586-2 is an anomalous object or entity suspended in the center of SCP-1586-1's womb that appears to be a perfect black body in the shape of a twelve-week-old human fetus.

1) How do they tell it's perfectly black? Making an incision into the uterine wall would let the gravity "leak out". And while I'm no medical doctor, I don't think that there's any camera-plus-light that can be simply pushed through a woman's cervix.

2) If its perfectly black, couldn't they at most say that it's silhouette matches that of a fetus?

and embedded agents at the hospital

Holy crap, the Foundation has agents embedded everywhere!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neutral vote because I love the idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this.
Could possibly stand to be fleshed out a little more, but as it stands the implications are strange and disturbing and that is always a good thing.
Upvoted.

One minor thing:
"SCP-1586-2 is an anomaly object or entity"

Not sure if you mean "Anomalous object or entity" or "anomaly, object, or entity" - no big deal, just a grammar fix.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another one with a good concept, but bad execution. I feel this could be much more interesting, though it does seem similar to SCP-007. If you expanded this idea, and added enough information to make it feel unique, I think it would be a lot better. For now I'll hold my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1586 came to the Foundation's attention on █/██/08 following [DATA EXPUNGED].

Don't like this.

The rest I do like, though. "Pregnant with a black hole" isn't too similar to 231 or the abdominal planet for me. +1'ed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, Scantron, it takes a classical two-body system…

Regardless, I like this for the imagery it presents, but the execution is a bit shoddy. Needs more mystery, less danger, in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really good concept, but as has been said, this execution is just a cross between Abdominal Planet and 231.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a premise with plenty of potential, but the execution lacks substance. It just nds to have something to make it memorable in a way that the SCPs others have mentioned didn't use.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this. Execution was fine, but it just wasn't interesting enough to separate it from the similar ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not really grabbing me, seconding what Scantron said.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've got some good, weird imagery what with a babby black hole, but instead of building on that weirdness/potential body horror, you amp up the danger instead. This could be a lot better than it is, and I encourage you to keep working on it, but this current form is rather underwhelming.

Might be worthwhile to read up some more on the effects of black holes and try and incorporate some elements that would be analogous to those effects.

EDIT: I feel like there's potentially good stuff hiding behind that [DATA EXPUNGED]. Just how do you get impregnated with a black hole?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh. I like the abdominal planet more, and this feels kinda like that + 231.

Not grabbing me, I'm afraid.
